blocks.content: The harmonic mean p-value (HMP) is a statistical technique for addressing the multiple comparisons problem that controls the strong-sense family-wise error rate). It improves on the power of Bonferroni correction by performing combined tests, i.e. by testing whether groups of p-values are statistically significant, like Fisher's method. However, similar to other extensions of Fisher's method, it avoids the restrictive assumption

source_span.quote: The harmonic mean p-value (HMP) is a statistical technique for addressing the multiple comparisons problem that controls the strong-sense family-wise error rate). It improves on the power of Bonferroni correction by performing combined tests, i.e. by testing whether groups of p-values are statistically significant, like Fisher's method. However, similar to other extensions of Fisher's method, it avoids the restrictive assumption

blocks.content: When \alpha is small (e.g. \alpha), the following multilevel test based on direct interpretation of the HMP controls the strong-sense family-wise error rate at level approximately \alpha:

source_span.quote: When \alpha is small (e.g. \alpha), the following multilevel test based on direct interpretation of the HMP controls the strong-sense family-wise error rate at level approximately \alpha:

blocks.content:
Define the HMP of any subset \mathcal{R} of the L p-values to be
\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R} = \frac{\sum_{i\in\mathcal{R}} w_{i}}{\sum_{i\in\mathcal{R}} w_{i}/p_{i}}.

source_span.quote:
# Define the HMP of any subset \mathcal{R} of the L p-values to be
\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R} = \frac{\sum_{i\in\mathcal{R}} w_{i}}{\sum_{i\in\mathcal{R}} w_{i}/p_{i}}.

blocks.content: Reject the null hypothesis that none of the p-values in subset \mathcal{R} are significant if \overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}\leq\alpha\,w_\mathcal{R}, where…

source_span.quote: # Reject the null hypothesis that none of the p-values in subset \mathcal{R} are significant if \overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}\leq\alpha\,w_\mathcal{R}, where w_\mathcal{R}=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{R}}w_i. (Recall that, by definition, \sum_{i=1}^L w_i=1.)

blocks.content: An asymptotically exact version of the above replaces \overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}in step 2 with p_{\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}} = \max\left\{\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}, w_{\mathcal{R}} \int_{w_{\mathcal{R}}/\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}}^\infty f_\textrm{Landau}\left(x\,|\,\log L +0.874,\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x\right\}, where L gives the number of p-values, not just those in subset \mathcal{R}.

source_span.quote: An asymptotically exact version of the above replaces \overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}in step 2 with p_{\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}} = \max\left\{\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}, w_{\mathcal{R}} \int_{w_{\mathcal{R}}/\overset{\circ}{p}_\mathcal{R}}^\infty f_\textrm{Landau}\left(x\,|\,\log L +0.874,\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x\right\}, where L gives the number of p-values, not just those in subset \mathcal{R}.

blocks.content: Since direct interpretation of the HMP is faster, a two-pass procedure may be used to identify subsets of p-values that are likely to be significant using direct interpretation, subject to confirmation using the asymptotically exact formula.

source_span.quote: Since direct interpretation of the HMP is faster, a two-pass procedure may be used to identify subsets of p-values that are likely to be significant using direct interpretation, subject to confirmation using the asymptotically exact formula.

blocks.content: The HMP has a range of properties that arise from generalized central limit theorem.

source_span.quote: The HMP has a range of properties that arise from generalized central limit theorem.

blocks.content: If the distributions of the p-values under the alternative hypotheses follow Beta distributions with parameters \left(0, a form considered by Sellke, Bayarri and Berger, then the inverse proportionality between the model-averaged Bayes factor and the HMP can be formalized as\overline{\textrm{BF}}=\sum_{i=1}^L \mu_i\,\textrm{BF}_i=\sum_{i=1}^L \mu_i\,\xi_i\,p_i^{\xi_i-1}\approx\bar\xi\sum_{i=1}^L w_i\,p_i^{-1}=\frac{\bar\xi}{\overset{\circ}{p}}, where

source_span.quote: If the distributions of the p-values under the alternative hypotheses follow Beta distributions with parameters \left(0, a form considered by Sellke, Bayarri and Berger, then the inverse proportionality between the model-averaged Bayes factor and the HMP can be formalized as\overline{\textrm{BF}}=\sum_{i=1}^L \mu_i\,\textrm{BF}_i=\sum_{i=1}^L \mu_i\,\xi_i\,p_i^{\xi_i-1}\approx\bar\xi\sum_{i=1}^L w_i\,p_i^{-1}=\frac{\bar\xi}{\overset{\circ}{p}}, where

blocks.content:
*\mu_i is the prior probability of alternative hypothesis i, such that \sum_{i=1}^L\mu_i=1,
*\xi_i/(1+\xi_i) is the expected value of p_i under alternative hypothesis i,
*w_i=u_i/\bar\xi is the weight attributed to p-value i,
*u_i = \left(\mu_i\,\xi_i\right)^{1/(1-\xi_i)} incorporates the prior model probabilities and powers into the weights, and
*\bar\xi = \sum_{i=1}^L u_i normalizes the weights.

source_span.quote:
*\mu_i is the prior probability of alternative hypothesis i, such that \sum_{i=1}^L\mu_i=1,
*\xi_i/(1+\xi_i) is the expected value of p_i under alternative hypothesis i,
*w_i=u_i/\bar\xi is the weight attributed to p-value i,
*u_i = \left(\mu_i\,\xi_i\right)^{1/(1-\xi_i)} incorporates the prior model probabilities and powers into the weights, and
*\bar\xi = \sum_{i=1}^L u_i normalizes the weights.

blocks.content: The approximation works best for well-powered tests (\xi_i\ll 1).

source_span.quote: The approximation works best for well-powered tests (\xi_i\ll 1).

blocks.content: For likelihood ratio tests with exactly two degrees of freedom, Wilks' theorem implies that p_i=1/R_i, where R_i is the maximized likelihood ratio in favour of alternative hypothesis i, and therefore \overset{\circ}{p}=1/\bar{R}, where \bar{R} is the weighted mean maximized likelihood ratio, using weights w_1,\dots,w_L. Since R_i is an upper bound on the Bayes factor, \textrm{BF}_i, then 1/\overset{\circ}{p} is an upper bound on the model-averaged Bayes factor:\overline{\textrm{BF}}\leq\frac{1}{\overset{\circ}{p}}.While the equivalence holds only for two degrees of freedom, the relationship between \overset{\circ}{p} and \bar{R}, and therefore \overline{\textrm{BF}}, behaves similarly for other degrees of freedom.

source_span.quote: For likelihood ratio tests with exactly two degrees of freedom, Wilks' theorem implies that p_i=1/R_i, where R_i is the maximized likelihood ratio in favour of alternative hypothesis i, and therefore \overset{\circ}{p}=1/\bar{R}, where \bar{R} is the weighted mean maximized likelihood ratio, using weights w_1,\dots,w_L. Since R_i is an upper bound on the Bayes factor, \textrm{BF}_i, then 1/\overset{\circ}{p} is an upper bound on the model-averaged Bayes factor:\overline{\textrm{BF}}\leq\frac{1}{\overset{\circ}{p}}.While the equivalence holds only for two degrees of freedom, the relationship between \overset{\circ}{p} and \bar{R}, and therefore \overline{\textrm{BF}}, behaves similarly for other degrees of freedom.
